docs.title: OpenCrate BMS Docs
docs.text:
OpenCrate is an open-source building management system built in Rust. The product combines a protocol layer, an application platform, a web/API surface, and an operator UI into one deployable system.

Use these docs to understand how the platform is put together and how the main subsystems fit.

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• Read Getting Started for the fastest path to a local instance.
  • Read Architecture for the high-level platform shape.
  • Read Features for the major operator-facing capabilities.
  • Read Deployment for packaging and runtime expectations.
  • Read Integrations for protocols and external connectors.
  • Read Developer Notes for the codebase map and extension points.

docs.title: Platform at a glance
docs.text:
OpenCrate currently centers around a Rust application with these major layers:

  • Protocol bridges for BACnet and Modbus, with shared abstractions under src/protocol/ and src/bridge/
  • Application services for alarms, schedules, history, logic, reporting, notifications, MQTT, webhook delivery, and FDD
  • SQLite-backed stores under src/store/ for persisted configuration and runtime history
  • An Axum HTTP API and WebSocket stream under src/api/
  • A Dioxus-based operator UI under src/gui/

docs.title: Product shape
docs.text:
The codebase currently supports:

  • Device discovery and commissioning
  • Point history, schedules, alarms, and acknowledgements
  • Notification routing, webhook delivery, and MQTT publishing
  • Reporting, energy tracking, and fault detection
  • Desktop and API-enabled deployments from one Rust workspace

These docs focus on the current architecture reflected in this repository rather than a future roadmap.

Last synced: 2026-03-31

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text: OpenCrate is organized around a central platform state shared across protocol services, persistence, the API layer, and the operator UI.

docs.title: Major subsystems
docs.text:
• src/platform.rs creates the long-lived runtime services and shared handles
  • src/store/ contains SQLite-backed persistence for configuration, runtime history, and feature modules
  • src/event/ distributes internal events used by notifications, MQTT, webhooks, and other automation
  • src/api/ exposes REST and WebSocket endpoints when the api feature is enabled
  • src/gui/ contains the Dioxus desktop and web UI components
  • src/bridge/, src/protocol/, and protocol-specific modules handle field integration

docs.title: Architectural style
docs.text:
The project is intentionally modular rather than microservice-based:

  • one deployable Rust application
  • many feature-oriented modules
  • explicit persistence per domain
  • event-driven reactions for cross-cutting concerns

That keeps local development and self-hosted deployment simple while still letting features evolve independently.

docs.title: Read next
docs.text:
• Platform Overview
  • API and Realtime
  • Data and Persistence

docs.title: HTTP surface
docs.text:
When the api feature is enabled, OpenCrate exposes an Axum-based API rooted at /api/.

The current API surface includes endpoints for:

  • auth and first-run setup
  • points, alarms, and discovery data
  • reporting and exports
  • energy and FDD features
  • webhook and notification configuration

docs.title: Realtime updates
docs.text:
The WebSocket endpoint provides live updates for operator-facing screens and automation clients that need state changes without polling.

Internally, that realtime layer is fed by the same platform and event-bus updates used by other background services.

docs.title: Auth model
docs.text: Authentication is handled in the application layer and guarded by role-based permissions. API routes are grouped by domain and permission requirements under src/api/routes/.

docs.title: Why the split matters
docs.text:
The API layer is deliberately thin:

  • route handlers parse requests and validate access
  • stores and domain modules perform the actual work
  • shared state keeps UI and API behavior aligned

That makes it easier to add new routes without re-implementing business logic.

docs.title: Store model
docs.text:
Most major features in OpenCrate have their own store under src/store/. Each store owns its schema, migrations, and CRUD/query helpers for one domain.

Examples include:

  • alarms
  • reporting
  • notifications
  • commissioning
  • MQTT
  • energy
  • FDD
  • export

docs.title: Why SQLite per domain works here
docs.text:
OpenCrate is optimized for self-hosted deployments and local-first development. SQLite keeps packaging simple while still supporting a fairly rich application model.

Benefits of the current approach:

  • no external database required
  • transactional writes for config and runtime history
  • predictable deployment footprint
  • feature modules can evolve their schema independently

docs.title: Event-driven reactions
docs.text: Persistence is only part of the story. Many writes emit or feed events that other subsystems consume, which is how alarms can trigger notifications, webhooks, and MQTT publishes without hard-coding those concerns into every store.

docs.title: Developer takeaway
docs.text:
When you need to understand a feature, start with its store and then follow:

  1. the route or UI component that calls it
  2. any event-bus subscribers that react to it
  3. any background worker that schedules or retries work

docs.title: Runtime composition
docs.text:
The platform layer is responsible for constructing the shared runtime used everywhere else in the application.

The rough startup flow is:

  1. Load configuration and project context
  2. Open stores and run schema initialization
  3. Build shared state objects and event channels
  4. Start background workers and schedulers
  5. Expose UI and API layers against the same platform state

docs.title: Why this matters
docs.text: This design means features such as notifications, reporting, MQTT publishing, and FDD can react to the same underlying events and stores instead of duplicating transport or persistence logic.

docs.title: Key code areas
docs.text:
• src/platform.rs
  • src/lib.rs
  • src/event/
  • src/store/

docs.title: Cross-cutting services
docs.text:
Several features are wired as subscribers or workers around the same core runtime:

  • notification routing
  • webhook dispatch
  • MQTT publishing
  • reporting and export jobs
  • discovery and commissioning state
  • weather and energy services

That shared-runtime approach is one of the main reasons the codebase stays navigable even as feature count grows.

docs.title: Main deployment shapes
docs.text:
OpenCrate currently supports:

  • desktop app with embedded web server
  • API-only server deployment
  • web-target client builds alongside an API backend

docs.title: Packaging assumptions
docs.text: The project is designed to run with a project directory containing configuration, profiles, and runtime data. That makes upgrade and backup workflows simpler because the binary and the project data are separate concerns.

docs.title: Reverse proxy and TLS
docs.text: For production-style deployments, place OpenCrate behind a reverse proxy for TLS termination. The repository already includes proxy examples under deploy/.

docs.title: Operational concerns
docs.text:
A production-minded deployment should think about:

  • backups of the project data directory
  • log capture and retention
  • port exposure and CORS configuration
  • secret handling for API auth, webhook providers, SMTP, and similar integrations

docs.title: Current tradeoffs
docs.text: The system favors a simple single-node deployment over distributed infrastructure. That keeps setup approachable, but it also means scaling strategies are currently based on one application instance per project.

docs.title: Codebase map
docs.text:
If you are orienting yourself in the repository, start with this flow:

  1. README.md for product shape and build modes
  2. src/lib.rs for top-level module wiring
  3. src/platform.rs for runtime initialization
  4. src/store/ for feature persistence
  5. src/api/routes/ and src/gui/components/ for user-facing entry points

docs.title: Good first navigation paths
docs.text:
• Want to understand alarms: start in src/store/, then src/notification/, src/webhook/, and related UI/API routes
  • Want to understand protocols: start in src/protocol/ and src/bridge/
  • Want to understand the UI: start in src/gui/app.rs, src/gui/state.rs, and the feature views

docs.title: Writing docs going forward
docs.text: Only pages under docs/site/ are intended for public publishing. Internal notes like roadmap items and design specs should stay outside that subtree unless they are explicitly rewritten for public docs.

docs.title: Publishing flow
docs.text: This private repo is the authoring source for public docs. A GitHub Action syncs docs/site/ into opencrate-site, where the public Pages build assembles the landing page and /docs/ section together.

docs.title: Extending the platform
docs.text: For a practical feature development sequence, read Adding a Feature.

docs.title: Recommended path
docs.text:
Most new feature work in OpenCrate goes more smoothly if you add it in this order:

  1. domain types and persistence
  2. runtime wiring in platform/shared state
  3. API routes if the feature needs remote access
  4. UI views if the feature is operator-facing
  5. event subscribers or schedulers if it reacts asynchronously

docs.title: Why this order works
docs.text: The application is organized around stable domain modules and shared runtime state. If you begin with a UI or route before the store and runtime shape are clear, you usually end up backtracking.

docs.title: Common touch points
docs.text:
• src/store/ for schema and persistence
  • src/platform.rs for runtime registration
  • src/api/routes/ for HTTP exposure
  • src/gui/components/ for operator workflows
  • src/event/ for cross-cutting reactions

docs.title: Core Features
docs.text: OpenCrate has grown into a feature-rich BMS platform. The easiest way to understand it is by grouping capabilities into operator workflows.

docs.title: Device onboarding
docs.text:
• protocol bridges and discovery flows find devices and points
  • commissioning tools track verification and sign-off work
  • templates and profiles help standardize accepted devices

docs.title: Operations
docs.text:
• alarms support active views, history, acknowledgements, shelving, and routing
  • schedules support weekly and exception behavior with BACnet schedule sync
  • history collection supports interval and change-based trending
  • floor plans and site maps provide spatial context for equipment and points

docs.title: Automation and analysis
docs.text:
• a visual logic system compiles into executable logic
  • reporting turns runtime data into scheduled reports and exports
  • energy features track meters, rates, baselines, and rollups
  • FDD rules detect common system issues and maintain fault history

docs.title: Integrations and delivery
docs.text:
• MQTT publishing distributes event and value updates
  • webhook providers deliver external notifications and integrations
  • email, SMS, and webhook channels support routed alarm delivery

docs.title: User model
docs.text:
• authentication and role-based permissions gate administrative actions
  • audit logging records operator actions across major workflows

docs.title: Logic engine
docs.text: OpenCrate includes a visual logic system that compiles user-authored blocks into executable logic. The goal is to keep editing approachable while still running a real scriptable automation layer under the hood.

docs.title: Reporting
docs.text:
Reporting turns stored operational data into scheduled output. The reporting subsystem pulls from alarms, history, runtime summaries, and newer domain modules like energy.

Important traits of the current design:

  • report definitions are persisted and schedulable
  • rendering happens inside the application runtime
  • delivery can reuse the same notification-oriented infrastructure patterns

docs.title: Energy
docs.text:
The energy module adds domain-specific structure on top of normal trend and schedule data:

  • meters
  • utility rates
  • baseline records
  • cost and demand summaries

This is a good example of how the platform can grow feature depth without changing the overall runtime model.

docs.title: FDD
docs.text:
Fault detection and diagnostics sits beside, not inside, the basic alarm engine. It evaluates rule logic against point data, tracks active faults, and emits events that other systems can react to.

That separation is useful because:

  • standard alarms stay simple and immediate
  • higher-order diagnostics can evolve independently
  • outbound integrations can treat faults as another event stream

docs.title: Discovery to acceptance
docs.text:
The operations lifecycle starts with discovery:

  • scan BACnet or Modbus networks
  • inspect discovered devices and points
  • accept what belongs in the project
  • apply names, templates, and tags

That flow bridges the protocol layer and the project model. It is one of the most important places where raw field data becomes an operator-friendly system.

docs.title: Day-to-day operation
docs.text:
Once a project is commissioned, operators mainly live in:

  • alarm views
  • point and equipment pages
  • schedule editors
  • history and dashboards
  • floor plans and site maps

Those screens are all backed by the same core stores and realtime updates, which keeps the desktop and API-backed experiences aligned.

docs.title: Configuration and accountability
docs.text:
OpenCrate treats administrative actions as first-class operational data:

  • notification rules
  • webhook targets
  • user roles
  • commissioning status
  • sign-off events
  • audit trail entries

That matters because BMS changes are rarely isolated. Alarm routing, schedules, device config, and sign-off workflows all need traceability.

docs.title: Local run options
docs.text:
OpenCrate can be run in a few different shapes depending on what you want to validate:

  • cargo run --features desktop,api for the all-in-one desktop app with embedded web server
  • cargo build --release --features api for an API server deployment
  • cargo run --features desktop for desktop UI only

The current repository README covers the quickest commands and expected ports.

docs.title: What boots with the app
docs.text:
At startup, the platform initializes core services and shared state, then conditionally starts UI and API surfaces based on enabled features.

The important runtime concerns are:

  • project loading
  • store initialization and migrations
  • event bus startup
  • background services like notification routing, reporting, export tasks, and protocol tasks
  • UI/API state wiring

docs.title: Project data layout
docs.text:
A project directory typically contains:

  • scenario.json for scenario-level settings
  • profiles/ for profile JSON
  • data/ for runtime SQLite databases and generated secrets

This separation lets the binary remain portable while project state lives outside the compiled application.

docs.title: Recommended first reading
docs.text:
After you have the app running, read:

  1. Architecture Overview
  2. Core Features
  3. Deployment Model

docs.title: Field protocols
docs.text:
The codebase already includes first-class support for:

  • BACnet/IP, BACnet/SC, and BACnet MS/TP related flows
  • Modbus TCP and RTU related flows

Those integrations are organized so transport details stay close to the protocol layer while the rest of the platform works against more stable internal abstractions.

docs.title: External delivery and messaging
docs.text:
OpenCrate can also publish or deliver events outward through:

  • MQTT
  • webhooks
  • email and SMS channels for notifications

docs.title: Design approach
docs.text:
The integration design is intentionally layered:

  • field protocols bring data into the platform
  • stores persist the important state
  • event-driven delivery pushes meaningful changes back out

That separation is what lets new outbound integrations reuse existing alarm, history, and fault events instead of reinventing source logic.

docs.title: BACnet
docs.text: BACnet support is one of the core reasons this project exists. The repository already includes support for BACnet/IP, BACnet/SC, and MS/TP related workflows, along with discovery, schedule sync, trend interactions, and other controller-facing features.

docs.title: Modbus
docs.text: Modbus support complements BACnet for simpler devices and meters. The platform treats both protocols as inputs into the same higher-level node, history, alarm, and commissioning workflows.

docs.title: Why protocol abstraction matters
docs.text: OpenCrate tries to keep protocol specifics close to the bridge and protocol modules while letting the rest of the application reason about points, devices, equipment, and events. That is what makes protocol-agnostic features like commissioning or dashboards possible.

docs.title: Notifications
docs.text: Alarm routing can deliver through multiple channels, including webhook, email, and SMS flows. Those channels all sit behind a shared routing concept so operators configure recipients and rules rather than hard-coded per-feature destinations.

docs.title: Webhooks
docs.text: Webhook delivery gives external systems a simple way to react to alarms, FDD events, and similar state changes. This keeps OpenCrate interoperable with lightweight automation platforms and custom integrations.

docs.title: MQTT
docs.text: MQTT publishing is useful when you want OpenCrate to feed dashboards, data pipelines, or external automation tools. It turns the internal event stream into structured publishes against configurable topic patterns.

docs.title: Integration pattern
docs.text:
Across notifications, webhooks, and MQTT, the recurring design is:

  1. detect or persist an important state change
  2. emit an internal event
  3. let specialized delivery modules fan that event outward
